topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The office felt cavernous, the silence a heavy, unexpected blanket where Dr. Alistair’s booming laughter used to resonate. I stood by the door, my hand hovering over the knob, a strange, hollow sensation blooming in my chest. The framed photograph on his desk, the one from the conference in Kyoto, seemed impossibly bright under the fluorescent lights. I took a slow, deliberate breath, the air tasting thin. He was gone. Not just on vacation, but… vanished. I finally turned, my gaze sweeping over the empty chair, a slow, almost reverent movement. The sheer scale of what had occurred hit me, a sudden, dizzying drop. I felt the need to touch something, anything, to anchor myself to the reality that had suddenly shifted.

[story 2]

"So, that’s it?" I managed, the words coming out reedy and unfamiliar. My palms were slick against the cool mahogany of the mentor's desk. He hadn't left a note, no formal announcement, just a neatly folded, crisp business card on his blotter, dated for a future that clearly didn't include me. I picked it up, turning the heavy paper over and over, the texture almost vibrating beneath my fingertips. A small, involuntary sound escaped my throat—a soft, breathy exhale. My eyes darted around the room, cataloging the familiar clutter, searching for a misplaced sign, a hidden message, anything to explain this profound discontinuity. The world seemed to tilt slightly on its axis.

[story 3]

I walked into his study, the door clicking shut behind me with an almost alarming finality. The room smelled of aged paper and faint pipe tobacco, scents that had been the backdrop to my entire professional life. There, on the leather armchair he always occupied, was only a neatly pressed tweed jacket draped over the back. It was an absence so stark, so complete, that I couldn't move. My jaw tightened, a physical clenching in my neck. I let my shoulders slump, the tension draining out of me until I felt strangely light, almost buoyant. I walked toward the window, staring out at the cityscape, feeling the vastness of the unknown spread before me, and a slow, wide smile spread across my face, utterly unbidden.
